question: Quality in operations management has two core dimensions: 'quality of design' and 'quality of conformance.' What is the difference?
options: They are the same concept described from different perspectives

Quality of design: how well the product's specifications match what customers want (a Porsche is designed to higher quality standards than a Honda Civic — different design quality targets); Quality of conformance: how consistently the product is manufactured to its design specifications (a Civic with no defects has perfect conformance quality; a Porsche with defects has poor conformance quality even though it's a better design); a product can fail on either dimension: poor design (specs don't meet customer needs) or poor conformance (can't reliably build to spec)
Quality of conformance is always more important than quality of design
Quality only has one relevant dimension in operations management
explanation: Design quality vs. conformance quality distinction: Rolls-Royce has higher design quality targets than Toyota Corolla (more features, better materials, higher performance specs). But Toyota has excellent conformance quality — almost every Corolla meets its specifications. A Rolls-Royce with unreliable electrical systems has high design quality but poor conformance. The operations manager primarily controls conformance quality (process capability). The product manager controls design quality (specifications). Confusing the two leads to: designing products customers want but building them inconsistently (design quality success, conformance failure) or building products perfectly to spec but specs don't match needs (conformance success, design failure).

question: Six Sigma is a quality standard that means:
options: The process produces six defects per million opportunities

3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO) — representing a process so capable that the defect rate is statistically equivalent to being 6 standard deviations from the mean; this is effectively near-zero defects (99.99966% accuracy); achieved through DMAIC methodology (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) to systematically reduce process variation; originally developed at Motorola (1986), famously adopted by GE (Jack Welch), and now a standard quality management approach across industries
Process capability is six times better than competitors
Quality at the six highest levels of performance
explanation: Six Sigma: at 1 sigma (68% accuracy), a hospital would make 25 wrong drug prescriptions per 100. At 3 sigma (99.73%), 270 wrong prescriptions per 100,000 doses. At 6 sigma (99.99966%), 3.4 wrong prescriptions per million doses. The aviation industry typically operates at 6+ sigma for safety-critical systems — why: the number of potential failure points means that 99% reliability on each component results in a system failure probability unacceptably high when multiplied across thousands of components. DMAIC: the systematic problem-solving process for reaching Six Sigma quality levels.

question: Total Quality Management (TQM) differs from traditional quality inspection in that TQM:
options: TQM simply uses more inspectors at the end of the production line

TQM is a philosophy of building quality in throughout the entire organization rather than inspecting it out at the end: everyone (not just the QC department) is responsible for quality; quality is designed into products and processes (not inspected afterward); continuous improvement is built into daily work; customer focus defines quality standards; supplier quality is managed; statistical methods identify and prevent defects; traditional inspection: QC team inspects finished products and rejects defects; TQM: design, production, procurement, and service teams all prevent defects from occurring
TQM applies only to manufacturing and cannot be used in service industries
TQM means that all workers must be trained in statistical methods regardless of their role
explanation: TQM originated from Deming's work in Japan (1950s) and became widely adopted after Japan's manufacturing quality revolution. Deming's 14 Points include: cease dependence on inspection, drive out fear (workers who fear admitting defects create hidden quality problems), break down barriers between departments (quality failures often cross functional boundaries), and continuously improve. TQM vs. inspection: inspection is defensive and corrective (catch what went wrong); TQM is preventive (don't let it go wrong). The financial case: prevention costs (training, statistical process control, process design) are almost always less than failure costs (scrap, rework, warranty, customer defection).

question: Statistical Process Control (SPC) uses control charts to monitor process output. What is the purpose of control limits on a control chart?
options: To set the maximum and minimum specifications the customer will accept

To distinguish between common cause variation (natural, random variation inherent to the process) and special cause variation (unusual events requiring investigation and correction); when measurements fall within control limits, the process is 'in control' (only natural variation) and workers should NOT intervene (over-adjustment makes things worse); when measurements fall outside control limits, a special cause has occurred and should be investigated; this prevents the error of adjusting a process for every natural variation (making it worse) while ensuring real problems are investigated
To show when workers are making mistakes in their individual performance
To set pricing ranges for finished products based on production quality
explanation: SPC distinction (Shewhart): common cause variation (the system is working normally but natural variation occurs — acceptable) vs. special cause variation (something unusual happened — investigate). The control limits are set at ±3 standard deviations from the mean — if the process is normally distributed, 99.73% of output falls within these limits by chance. A point outside = only 0.27% probability of natural occurrence → almost certainly a special cause. The management implication: workers who adjust their process every time a measurement moves (without SPC guidance) are reacting to normal variation — making the process WORSE through over-adjustment. SPC tells you when to act and when to leave it alone.

question: The International Organization for Standardization's ISO 9001 certification requires organizations to:
options: Produce zero defects in all manufactured products

Document their quality management system, establish consistent processes, monitor performance against quality objectives, and continuously improve — ISO 9001 certifies that the company has systematic quality management processes in place, not that their products meet any specific performance standard; a company that consistently produces mediocre products through a well-documented process can be ISO 9001 certified; the value: buyers use ISO 9001 certification as a signal that a supplier has systematic processes and won't surprise them with unpredictable quality
Achieve Six Sigma defect rates across all processes
Only produce products that meet specific government-regulated quality standards
explanation: ISO 9001 misunderstanding: ISO 9001 is a management system standard (you have documented processes and improve them) not a product quality standard (your products have X performance). A company making average-quality products consistently can be ISO 9001 certified. A company making excellent products through chaotic, undocumented processes cannot. Why customers require ISO 9001 from suppliers: it provides assurance that the supplier won't have unpredictable quality failures — even if their product quality isn't world-class, at least it's consistent and the supplier knows how to identify and improve problems. In B2B purchasing (especially automotive, aerospace, medical devices), ISO 9001 is often a minimum qualification for supplier consideration.

question: Johnson & Johnson's 1982 Tylenol crisis: seven people died after someone laced Tylenol capsules with cyanide. J&J recalled 31 million bottles immediately. What quality management principle did J&J demonstrate, and what was the business outcome?
options: J&J's recall was forced by regulators, and the company suffered permanent market share loss

J&J demonstrated: (1) Putting customer safety unambiguously above short-term profit (the recall cost $100M at the time); (2) Transparent communication with customers and regulators; (3) Crisis response as a quality system test — how a company behaves during a quality failure is itself a quality signal; outcome: Tylenol, which held 37% of the analgesic market before the crisis, recovered to 35% within one year of the recall and went on to regain full market leadership; J&J's decisive response created more brand trust than the incident destroyed; contrast: companies that hide or minimize quality failures suffer more permanent damage
Immediate product recall always permanently destroys brand reputation
J&J's recall was unnecessary since the contamination was caused by a criminal, not a product defect
explanation: J&J Tylenol crisis and brand trust: the lesson is not just ethical but strategic. J&J CEO James Burke recalled all 31M bottles despite: (1) the contamination being a criminal act, not a manufacturing defect; (2) the recall costing $100M; (3) many advisors recommending a regional recall. The swift, unambiguous decision signaled: J&J's values system places customers first. Consumer response was the reverse of what short-term financial analysis would predict — customers trusted J&J MORE after the recall than before. The contrast: Volkswagen's Dieselgate (2015) delay and cover-up, Blue Bell ice cream's listeria recall delay — companies that minimize quality failures suffer larger and more permanent brand damage than those who respond decisively.

question: Toyota introduced the 'Andon cord' — a rope running along the production line that any worker can pull to stop the entire line when they spot a defect. Pulling the cord is costly (stops an $8M/hour production line). Why did Toyota make this a cultural norm rather than a rare emergency measure?
options: Toyota pulls the Andon cord only for severe safety defects, not quality issues

Stopping the line immediately prevents defect propagation: in traditional manufacturing (don't stop the line), a defect discovered at station 5 was 'flagged for rework' and the defective unit continued through all remaining 15 stations — each adding cost to a unit that would ultimately be scrapped or extensively reworked; with Andon, the defect is fixed at station 5 before it moves; the 'stop to fix' philosophy reveals process problems immediately: every line stop triggers root cause analysis; Toyota's culture: a line stop is not failure, it's quality knowledge acquisition; workers who don't pull the cord when they see a defect are the quality failure; the Andon cord institutionalized worker empowerment for quality and created Toyota's most powerful quality feedback mechanism
The Andon cord is only used in Japanese factories and not in Toyota's US facilities
Workers rarely use the Andon cord because the cost of line stoppages creates personal accountability pressure
explanation: Andon cord philosophy: jidoka (autonomation — automation with human judgment). Stop the process immediately when an abnormality occurs. The financial logic: a line running at $8M/hour with a known defect creates: rework labor at all subsequent stations, potential scrap cost of full-unit value, and customer delivery disruption if units must be recalled. Stopping for 5 minutes (cost: $667K at $8M/hour — but typically much less as lines restart quickly) to fix the root cause prevents these costs. Toyota lines are stopped 500–1,000 times per shift at high-quality assembly plants — each stop is a learning opportunity. The correlation: more Andon pulls = better quality, not worse. The stops are quality feedback data.

question: A medical device company discovers a manufacturing process defect that affects 0.1% of its glucose meters (1 in 1,000 units). Each defective unit could provide an incorrect blood glucose reading that could cause a diabetic patient to over- or under-dose insulin. The defect is detectable through an individual unit test. Cost of testing all units: $500,000. Cost of a potential patient harm lawsuit: $10M average. Expected units shipped with defect: 100 (0.1% of 100,000 units). What does quality economics suggest?
options: Test all units since testing is always required for medical devices

Expected value analysis with asymmetric risk: without testing: expected cost = 100 defective units × P(detection by patient and lawsuit) × $10M; even if only 10% of defective units lead to a lawsuit = 10 lawsuits × $10M = $100M expected loss; cost of testing = $500K; testing ROI = ($100M expected loss) / ($500K testing cost) = 200:1 return; beyond the financial calculation: medical device quality decisions involve FDA regulatory consequences (warning letters, consent decrees, mandatory recalls), patient safety obligations, and reputation costs not captured in lawsuit settlement value alone; the quality economics unambiguously support 100% testing
Testing is not cost-effective since only 100 units are defective
Medical devices are exempt from quality economics because they are fully regulated
explanation: Quality economics in medical devices: the cost of failure model must include probability of harm, regulatory consequences, and reputational damage. FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (Quality System Regulation) requires medical device manufacturers to have processes for detecting and correcting quality issues. But beyond regulatory compliance, the expected value calculation is decisive: $500K testing cost vs. expected losses from harm (financial + regulatory + reputational). This is why Medtronic, J&J, and other medical device companies test 100% of certain product categories even when statistically most units pass — the tail risk of harm is too high to manage through sampling only. Cost of quality = appraisal cost (testing) + prevention cost, weighed against failure cost.

question: Starbucks received widespread criticism in 2008 when Howard Schultz's internal memo leaked, describing how: automatic espresso machines, breakfast sandwiches warming in the store, and pre-packaged pastries had made stores smell like 'cheese and onions' instead of coffee. What quality management failure does this represent?
options: Starbucks failed to maintain food safety standards in its stores

Quality drift through incremental compromise: each individual operational decision (automatic espresso for speed, warm breakfast sandwiches for revenue, packaged pastries for efficiency) was made in isolation with a local optimization rationale (speed, margin, cost); no single decision looked like a quality failure; but the cumulative effect of multiple small compromises produced a qualitatively different customer experience — the 'Starbucks experience' (artisan coffee theater) had been replaced by fast food; this is 'quality by neglect' — no one actively decided to lower quality, but many decisions optimized for other metrics (cost, speed, margin) and quality was the unmeasured casualty
Automatic espresso machines always produce lower quality coffee than manual machines
Starbucks solved all quality issues by hiring more baristas
explanation: Quality drift: the Starbucks case is a classic study in how quality degrades through accumulated small compromises, each individually rational. The automatic espresso machine decision: faster service (speed metric improves), lower barista skill required (labor cost decreases), consistent espresso extraction (technical quality metric improves) — but the visible craft of barista making espresso (customer experience quality) is destroyed. The breakfast sandwich decision: revenue per visit increases (financial metric improves), but the smell compromises the coffee experience. Schultz's insight: quality metrics that don't capture the full customer experience are misleading. The lesson: quality must be defined from the customer's perspective and measured on the dimensions customers value, not just the dimensions that are easy to measure.

question: Amazon's third-party seller marketplace has a quality problem: among 2 million+ third-party sellers, a portion sell counterfeit or unsafe products. Amazon uses algorithmic monitoring to detect: unusually high return rates, negative review patterns, and seller location anomalies. What quality management approach is this?
options: Amazon has no quality management system for third-party sellers

Statistical process control applied to marketplace quality: Amazon monitors seller quality metrics (return rate, negative review rate, A-to-Z claim rate) against control limits; sellers exceeding threshold trigger automated investigation or suspension; this is SPC applied to a service marketplace rather than a manufacturing line; the challenge: Amazon cannot inspect each of millions of products before shipment (the equivalent would be inspecting finished goods); instead, it uses outcome metrics (customer returns, complaints) as proxies for product quality and detects outlier sellers whose patterns suggest quality failures; the system is imperfect (counterfeits slip through) but scales to millions of products where individual inspection would be impossible
Amazon inspects every product before it reaches a customer
Quality management cannot apply to marketplace platforms since Amazon doesn't make the products
explanation: Digital marketplace quality management: traditional quality management assumes the organization controls the production process. Amazon's marketplace: 60%+ of sales come from third-party sellers Amazon doesn't control. Quality management solution: data-driven monitoring of outcome metrics as quality proxies. A seller with 15% return rate when the category average is 3% is statistically an outlier — high probability of quality problem. Amazon's automated system: suspension of sellers above threshold + investigation. This is the digital equivalent of SPC — monitoring outputs (returns, complaints) to detect process failures (seller quality problems) without being able to inspect inputs (each product). Limitations: sophisticated counterfeiters can stay below detection thresholds.

question: A fast food chain discovers that customer complaint rates vary significantly across its 1,200 franchise locations: 5 locations have zero complaints, 1,180 have 2–8 complaints/month, and 15 locations have 40+ complaints/month. What quality management intervention addresses each group?
options: Apply identical quality improvement programs to all 1,200 locations

Pareto analysis (80/20 rule): 15 locations (1.25% of total) are likely responsible for a disproportionate share of total complaints; priority: (1) The 15 high-complaint locations: intensive investigation and correction — site visits, mystery shoppers, operations audits to identify specific process failures; (2) The 5 zero-complaint locations: study and replicate best practices — what are they doing differently? Then propagate those practices to the 1,180 middle locations; (3) The 1,180 middle locations: systematic training updates and standard process reinforcement; the strategic error would be applying the same program to all 1,200 equally — the Pareto principle says: fix the outlier failures first (highest ROI), learn from the outlier successes second
All locations require identical corrective action regardless of complaint rate
High-performing locations should not be studied since their practices may not transfer
explanation: Pareto/80-20 principle in quality management: Pareto observed that 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. In quality management: 80% of customer complaints typically come from 20% of locations/products/processes. The operational implication: concentrate quality improvement resources on the high-defect sources first — addressing 15 locations may resolve 60%+ of total chain complaints. Learning from the 5 zero-complaint locations: these may be practicing something that others aren't — study and replicate. The 1,180 middle locations: maintain through standard training and monitoring. This is resource-efficient quality management vs. spreading improvement effort equally across all locations.

question: A software company releases a new feature with a 'silent failure' mode: when an error occurs, the system logs the error internally but shows the user a success message. Six months after deployment, data analysis reveals the feature has a 12% failure rate that no customer ever reported. What quality management failure does this illustrate?
options: The 12% failure rate is acceptable since customers didn't complain

Voice of the customer vs. system data: traditional quality relied on customer complaints as failure signals; the silent failure mode suppressed the customer signal; quality management today requires: error logging and monitoring (the internal logs had the data — but no process was checking them), performance metrics for all features (defining and monitoring the intended success rate vs. actual), proactive monitoring rather than waiting for customer feedback; the fundamental failure: quality measurement was designed around customer visibility (complaints) rather than process capability (actual success rate); a 12% failure rate that customers can't see is a quality failure the organization doesn't know it has — and cannot fix
Silent failures are acceptable since customers experience success messages
Log monitoring is an IT concern, not a quality management concern
explanation: Silent quality failures: one of the most dangerous quality patterns in software. The customer experience (success message) and the actual outcome (12% silent failure) are disconnected. Quality management requires: (1) Defining what success actually means (not just 'user sees a success message' but 'the underlying operation completed successfully'); (2) Monitoring actual system behavior, not user-visible behavior; (3) Alert systems when metrics deviate from baseline. This is the digital equivalent of a car that shows 'engine OK' on the dashboard when the engine is actually failing. Quality in digital products requires: error rate monitoring, latency monitoring, data integrity validation — not just tracking whether error messages appear.

question: Deming's 14 Points for management include: 'Eliminate numerical quotas for the workforce' and 'Drive out fear so that everyone may work effectively.' How do numerical quotas and fear specifically damage quality?
options: Quotas and fear are effective quality management tools that Deming supported

Quotas damage quality: when workers have a daily output quota (100 units/day), they hit the quota by sacrificing quality rather than missing quota and facing consequences; a unit that 'counts' toward the quota but has a defect gets shipped; workers who catch defects are penalized for slowing output to fix them; the quota metric optimizes for quantity and creates perverse incentives against quality; Fear of reporting quality problems: workers who find defects but know that reporting slows production or reflects on their performance will hide defects; Toyota's Andon cord works because there's no fear of line stops — Deming's insight: quality failures are almost always system failures (bad processes), not worker failures; workers who fear punishment for system failures can't fix those failures
Numerical production quotas always improve quality by setting clear performance standards
Fear motivates workers to produce higher quality work through accountability
explanation: Deming's quality philosophy: 94% of problems are system problems, not people problems. Quotas create perverse incentives: a quota of 100 units/day means hitting 100 substandard units is 'success' and hitting 90 perfect units is 'failure.' Fear creates information suppression: workers who see a quality problem but know reporting it will cause delays (and their manager will be angry) learn to look away. The Toyota Andon cord cultural norm requires zero fear of reporting — management celebrates line stops as learning. Deming's insight: systems that punish quality problem reporters optimize for hiding problems rather than fixing them. Many corporate quality failures (Boeing 737 MAX engineer concerns suppressed, Wells Fargo fake account fraud) involve workers who knew but feared reporting.

question: A car manufacturer discovers its suppliers have varying quality levels: Supplier A delivers 0.1% defective parts; Supplier B delivers 2% defective; Supplier C delivers 0.01% defective. The purchasing team wants to drop Supplier B and consolidate to A and C for lower quality risk. The operations team resists. What is the operations team's concern?
options: The operations team wants to keep Supplier B for personal reasons

Supplier diversification vs. quality concentration: consolidating to only A and C creates: (1) Single source risk — if either supplier has a production problem, outage, or force majeure event, there's no alternative; Supplier B provides surge capacity and supply security; (2) Negotiating leverage — with 3 suppliers, the manufacturer can shift volume between them to negotiate price; with 2, leverage decreases; (3) Supply chain resilience — geographic, operational, or financial problems at A or C would require emergency sourcing; the operations team's position: the correct response to Supplier B's 2% defect rate is to work collaboratively to improve their process quality, not immediately drop them — the supply diversification value may outweigh the quality risk, especially if the manufacturer provides technical assistance to improve B's quality
The operations team is wrong — supplier quality should always override other considerations
Three suppliers is always better than two suppliers regardless of quality levels
explanation: Supplier quality vs. supply chain resilience tension: purchasing teams optimize for quality and cost; operations teams optimize for supply security. Both are right about their dimensions. The analysis: what is the cost of Supplier B's 2% defect rate (incoming inspection cost + rejection handling)? What is the cost of supply disruption if Supplier A has a fire, strike, or financial problem? Automotive JIT manufacturers learned in 2011 (Japan earthquake disrupted sole-source suppliers) that quality optimization through single sourcing creates catastrophic supply risk. The balanced approach: require Supplier B to achieve a quality improvement plan with milestones; maintain supply diversification until the decision must be made based on total cost including disruption risk.
